english: a medicinal herb = smug-čuṅ (smug chung) Med.
*srog daṅ pa-yo-tó-yo taṅ-te soṅ* (*srog dang pa yo to yo tang te song*) Ld. for srog daṅ bsdos (srog dang bsdos), v. sdo-ba (sdo ba).
(spelling doubtful, at any rate not pa-raṅ (pa rang)) n. of a mountain pass, 19 000 feet high, between Ladak and Spiti.
W., pá-ru (pa ru) C., B. 1. box, cylindrical or oval, high or flat, of wood or metal. — 2. pá-ru (pa ru), also pá-tra (pa tra) Sch. — 3. v. bá-ru (ba ru).
Sch. 'a teacher'; Lex.: n. of a Tibetan priest that went to China.

Bal., pág-gu (pag gu) Dzl., pau (pau) W., pág (pag) Glr., pau (pau) Wdn.: brick; pág-gu byéd-pa (pag gu byed pa) Dzl.; rñibs-pag (rnyibs pag) roof-tile Cs.; wá-pag (wa pag) gutter-tile Cs.; rdzá-pag (rdza pag), so-pag (so pag) Glr. burnt-brick Cs.; sá-pag (sa pag) Glr. unburnt-brick Cs.; pag(-bu)-mkhan (pag (-bu)-mkhan) mason Cs.; pag-rtsig (pag rtsig) brick-wall Cs.; *pag-tsir* (pag tsir) W. a row or layer of bricks; frq. used as a measure = a small span, *kha pag-tsir nyis yod* (kha pag tsir nyis yod) the snow is as deep as two layers of bricks. — Not quite plain is the etymology of ˌog-pag ('og pag), Lex.: ska-rágs-kyi rgyan (ska rags kyi rgyan), Sch.: 'a girdle ornamented with glass-beads'; and of pag-pór (pag por) Sch. cup or vessel with a lid.

Mil. also -po (-po) (cf. lpags (lpags)) 1. skin, hide; śu-ba (shu ba) to skin, acc. to Schr. also merely to fret the skin; págs-pai gos (pags pai gos) skin or fur-clothing S.g. — 2. foreskin, when the connection of words does not admit of a misconception, Mṅg. — 3. skin or peel of fruit, the bark of trees, also pags-śún (pags shun), and śuṅ-pags (shung pags); *pag-tág* (pag tag) C. bark-cord, match-cord; págs-ču (pags chu) anasarca, skin-dropsy; págs-ču - żugs (pags chu zhugs) affected with this disease.

resp. sku-paṅ (sku pang), 1. the bend or hollow formed by the belly and the thighs in sitting, lap, B., C., W.; paṅ-du soṅ (pang du song) he sat down on the lap of... Glr.; paṅ-khebs (pang khebs) apron; paṅ-khrag (pang khrag) the blood flowing off during child-birth; *paṅ-big* (pang big) W. urinary bladder; *paṅ-ri* (pang ri) (for dri (dri)?) *suṅ* (sung) C. she has the bloody flux; páṅ-yyog-ma (pang g.yog ma) Cs. midwife (a kinswoman generally has to officiate as such; a hired one receives a new dress for her services). — 2. the bend or hollow formed by the arm and the chest in carrying something; bosom, usually páṅ-pa (pang pa); śiṅ-páṅ-pa gaṅ (shing pang pa gang) an armful of wood; páṅ-par khyér-ba (pang par khyer ba) to carry (a child) on the arm Dzl. and elsewh.; sdóṅ-po páṅ-pas ma khyigs-pa tsam źig (sdong po pang pas ma khyigs pa tsam zhig) a tree not to be encompassed by a man's arms Pth.; *paṅ-gód* (pang god), *paṅ-kód* (pang kod) W. an armful.
1. W. an implement for stirring the fire; for scraping = rbad (rbad). — 2. Ts. = paṅ (pang).
seems to be the n. of a tree B., C.; Ssk. only: five.
C. = srin-bu pád-ma (srin bu pad ma), v. pád-ma (pad ma)..

Ssk. in C. pronounced *pé-ma* (pe ma) 1. water-lily, lotos, Nymphaea, if not nearer defined, the blue species, whilst the less frq. form pád-mo (pad mo) (acc. to Glr. fol. 62) seems to denote the white kind of this flower. — 2. (not in Ssk., at least acc. to Wls. and Williams, though Köpp. II, 61 seems to dissent): genitals, of either sex, Med. — 3. srin-bu pád-ma (srin bu pad ma) leech. — pad-kór (pad kor), pad-skór (pad skor) 1. a particular way of folding the fingers during prayer Cs. and Sch.; a certain gesture with the hand. 2. a kind of toupet of the women, also pad-ló (pad lo) C., W. — pád(-ma) dkár(-po) (pad (-ma) dkar (-po)) 1. white lotos. 2. title of a celebrated Sutra, translated by Burnouf, Was. (151). — pad-dkár źal-luṅ (pad dkar zhal lung) an astronomical work by Púgpapa, v. Cs. time-table. — pad-ma-čan (pad ma can) full of lotos; more particularly lotos-lake, with and without mtśo (mtsho) Glr. — pad-(ma daṅ nyi-ma daṅ) zlái gdan (pad -(ma dang nyi ma dang) zlai gdan) Glr. and elsewh., carpet with
